item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Small nitpick: It appears that there is a fear it could escape if it was able to get into the freight elevator, now I’m no expert but the article says 3390 weighs 70 tonnes and that seems like a very large amount, also it is said it fell through multiple floors to the one it is on now. I don’t doubt that the Foundation would have a pretty heavy duty freight elevator, but 70 tonnes would probably cause the elevator cables to snap and fall to the bottom of the shaft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is entirely through personal experience. I get called out for being too lolfoundation all the time, until now I always believed this was in reference to the canon, so perhaps I am a little bias.
None the less any silliness you may encounter in this article is unintentional and purely at your own discretion. But then again I am old so these things tend to bleed through every once and again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I will ever understand the SCP's almost fanatical hatred of humour

I'm not sure you understand the difference between humour (widely supported, enjoyed, and popular on the site) and silliness. lolFoundation is people doing stupid, unrealistic things in pursuit of cheap laughs — articles like my own SCP-1313 hinge on comedy value without breaking suspension of disbelief. 1313 is the most popular thing I've ever written; saying the site hates humour is proveably untrue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I will ever understand the SCP's almost fanatical hatred of humour, to the point where I have bore witness to inquisitional style witch-hunt downvotings of anything that might imply humour, regardless of how slight.
Since this article is devoid of all humour I'm going to presume you're talking about the Dolphin, let me explain something.
The universe in which the SCP exists is filled with things we do not understand if we could explain the origins of all we see then the Foundation would not exist, or its universe be half as enthralling. It may be that there is no explanation to the everything, there is no universal theory of everything, no one single origin story to what we contain, it simply is part of the chaos around us. It is because it is and it doesn't matter what we personally feel about that.
Chaos is the most terrifying thing as it is the most difficult concept for a person to grasp, it is bigger than us.
In short, to stab a man with a knife is expected, it is normal. To stab a man to death with an inflatable dolphin is chaos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The 2:30 pm section of the Level 1 access observation notes has some punctuation errors. The emails also have quite a few punctuation errors, but those can be excused as they are heat-of-the-moment, informal emails.

I enjoyed the concepts and logs in this skip at first, but as the article went on I personally felt that it began to devolve into silliness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: an earlier draft of 3390 had it pregnant and giving birth to an FT-17

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me go from liking this SCP to loving this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a massive wall of text, please stick it in a collapsable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the notes. I have replaced projectory with trajectory.
There is supposed to be a line which states the basement had flooded, I appear to have blacked that out for some reason, that was 2018, I was younger back then, less beardy, I replaced the submersible with a drone.

The reason the guy was not thrown a rope ladder or helped in anyway is unclear in the skip, I planned to do a SCP explained to explain why he was left there and I may as well do that now since I'll probably never get round to it.

+ show block
– hide block

before the SCP wiki SCP's were passed around like campfire stories, I saw my first SCP via an e-mail chain between a group of friends of mine who liked to exchange creepypasta, this was a different time of the internet you understand.
The first few I read entranced me and I was encouraged to re-write one and pass it on or write my own, to which I did.
I wrote a very basic and quite a cliche Huamiod SCP based on the Indian legend of the Naga. SCP-9893 (extra numbers for an extra edgy boi) was half human half snake (upper half human, lower half snake, though it occurs to me the other way around would be much more interesting) like all humanoid SCP's back then he was allowed limited movement around the site he was based at (site-99) and was effectively considered part of the Foundations staff. he was obsessive with creating the panacea, a cure for all disease (this was before SCP-500) and was told to be working almost constantly on its creation.
The skip ended with a series of security, O5 council, and psychological reports which painted SCP-9893 as something different, a psychopath who enjoyed hunting and killing various members of staff, typically picking out its targets several weeks in advance and building up a great deal of trust with the target before subjecting them to psychological and physical torture, sometimes for days.

Now the way 9893 did this was very interesting, you see I have two BIG fears and I decided to incorporate both these fears into 9893.
One is being buried alive, and 9893 would enjoy locking his targets within closets or lockers and burying them.
The other was being eaten alive. So yeah, he would devour his targets whole and would take great pleasure in keeping them trapped within him, listening to their pleas and laughing with excitement as their struggles slowly faded and died.
At this point, I did not know this was a sexual fetish.

I found out after I sent a second draft to a friend who stated to me over coffee in a very polite manner "I didn't know you were into Vore". By this point, several copies of my work had already been passed around, I was so proud of it.

Of course after some furious googling, yahoo'ing and asking Jeeves (again different time) I learned a lot about the Vore fetish and quickly omitted a very large part of SCP-9893.
I never finished 9893 and I pretty much lost the skip during one of the many computer changes I've had since then and it was only very recently I re-discovered the SCP foundation and wrote 3390.
But copies of 9893 may still exist…out there…somewhere.

The point of telling you all this is that 9893 has always remained, in my head, part of SCP canon, though it does not exist on this wiki it is out there, it did exist at some point and at least 20 people read it.
The researcher sending the emails in 3390 is SCP-9893, as he existed 12 years ago, a member of the foundation staff, whos excentric physco tendencies are tolerated as a good way of disposing of D-class personnel while maintaining a talented chemist on staff.
And he is now dead, 3390 killed him, mutilated his corpse and rammed a battery into his mouth.
To go into the detail the document did not, his eyes were removed, his teeth were removed, the lower part of his torso was flattened, his arms were bent back snapping the bone, and his spine was shattered in three regions, his fingernails were also removed and his hands were jammed into his eye sockets, a look of sheer terror plastered on his face.
He is dead and he is not coming back, therefore if my third greatest fear ever comes true, that someone finds an original copy of SCP-9893, we can at least sleep safe knowing it is Object Class: Terminated.

The Dolphin is a subtle hint at the thing which inspired 3390.

The French army was indeed fielding 20-year-old tanks at the start of WWII. The Char2C 3390 represents is called No.99 "Champagne", it and its 9 sisters were deployed as propaganda machines, a lot of films were made of them shooting at distant targets and shown to French audiences, once the war started to turn against the French an attempt to save all ten tanks was made by transporting them, via train, south. A fuel train caught fire on the line ahead of the transport and the decision was made to scuttle all ten tanks.
Champagne was the only survivor after its charges failed to detonate, it was captured by the Germans and displayed as a war trophy outside the Reichstag in Berlin until 1948 where it vanished.

Have a read of SCP-2956 if you want to know how SCP-3390 came to be.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

France also had around 1,800 FT-17's, tiny first world war tanks, that were still in service as mobile MG posts on the Maginot line or as patrol vehicles in various French colonies.
After Dunkirk and Case Yellow began (Germany's invasion of mid-south France) these outdated tanks were pushed into service, 1,500 were captured by the Germans and either handed over to Vichy France or kept in German service, 30 were sent to fight Partisans in Yugoslavia, around 200 saw service in Operation Barbosa, some survived to 1943, and around 100 were used in Normandy, mostly on the Atlantic wall, guarding airfields, or used to fill in gaps in support gaps in infantry divisions, most notably 716. & 312. Infanterie, and 16th Luftwaffe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few small things:

  • "Projectory" isn't a word, outside of basketball slang (so UrbanDictionary reveals). You mean "trajectory".
  • What's the submersible for? If the basement has been flooded, why not state that?
  • Seriously why not throw the emailing guy a rope ladder or something, even if he WAS the one who insulted the French? Blithely condemning low level staff to a hideous and easily preventable death for minor infractions is deep lolfoundation.

Also, is the dolphin just random, or is there a connection I'm just not getting? And was the French army actually fielding 20-year-old, hopelessly obsolete armour at the start of WW2, or has this thing just picked up the vibes and escaped from a museum?

That aside, I do kinda like the concept and article as a whole, and the edit where its ultimate intent is revealed by it breaking into the comms system and making an announcement is a good workaround. Though it likely wouldn't be a "terminal" it used (that's more for computer access), maybe an "emergency tannoy station" or "public address station" (or at least "PA terminal").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Admittedly it's been quite a while since I read the original, so it's hard to say how it compares to my first read-through. However, I can say there is has to have been some kind of improvement.

The style is well written. I kept my interest the whole way through. One last nitpick - It's vs. Its. Its is possessive, It's is a contraction of It Is. Correct that and I think you're good to go.

item.title: Re: PM
item.content:encoded: Ha Ha, sorry. I have no idea when people downvoted it only that they did. I just took a chance and mailed everyone. But thanks for coming back to check it out, sorry you didn't like it. I hope my next one will be better.

item.title: PM
item.content:encoded:
I negatively voted on this article early yesterday, May 1st. In the last hour, I received a PM:

Hi There,

I am messaging you because you gave SCP-3390 a negative rating.
This article has now been re-written and I hope you will take the time to read the new version.
I hope if you do you'll enjoy this new re-write.

If you don't, already down-voted it or simply don't care then feel free to ignore this message.

With love
-Mal

So I spent some time reading and re-evaluating it; Only to find, you haven't actually edited this since April 30th. (Facepalm)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll take that as a compliment, thanks man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you didn't tell me, I wouldn't have guessed this was a mini.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The new image is a 28mm model I bought from Warlord Games, placed in a painted shoebox and photo shopped to give it that grimy camera aesthetic.
I'm not the best at Photoshop so I apologise in advance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I have now replaced SCP-3390 with it's re-written version. I would like to thank all the people that helped me with this and apologise if I've ever come off as a bit of a dick.
Hopefully you'll find this new version a lot better than the old one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one a lot. Agreed with others that not much mysteries is in for this one, but +1 regardless.

item.title: Re: This is a good idea, but...
item.content:encoded: No problem and thanks, sorry to be such a bother about this.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
As Cimmerian stated previously, unless you can provide an original source for the photo on the card, it will not be allowed to be used. All images must be sourced and complient with no exceptions, harsh as it may seem. Whilst it may be true the image is fine to use, unless we can verify that then it cannot be used.

Send me or Cimmerian a PM if you wish to discuss this further.

item.title: Re: This is a good idea, but...
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to speak to ONE staff member who has the authority to make a final decision.

I believe Doctor Cimmerian from this post: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-2416403/scp-3390#post-3559494 would be the ideal person to contact regarding this.

item.title: Re: This is a good idea, but...
item.content:encoded:
This I understand.
I would be happy to discuss the issue further and re-submit an image I have full confidence complies as not under copyright but I'd like to speak to ONE staff member who has the authority to make a final decision. The last time things got a little…dramatic.

item.title: Re: This is a good idea, but...
item.content:encoded:
after an over reaction from the staff regarding a potential copyright issue of a 100 year old postcard

That wasn't an overreaction. Lawsuits arising from copyright misuse can get downright nasty, and certain conpanies have been known to try and sue a daycare center for the horrible copyright violation of... painting their characters on the walls of the playroom without written permission.

In other words, every photo needs to be squeaky-clean in terms of compliance, or the site can potentially get shut down over a legal snafu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The self-regeneration capabilities are mentioned in the second last paragraph before the experimentation notes begin.
I tried to emulate the format of the logs based on what I'd read in other SCP's but I'd appreciate an example of a different format you would feel would be less confusing.

I agree that more "mystery" needs to be added to this SCP. My original intention was to have one experiment possible reference was to be made somewhere in the document about Nazi experiments of infusing technology with human brains, in this case the brain of a french orphan child but in the end I thought that too cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people like the footnote, others think it's stupid. It's about half and half right now but I'll probably remove it.

item.title: Re: This is a good idea, but...
item.content:encoded:
It's funny that this would be the line you would choose to start your argument with. This line originally read "SCP-3390 will enter its active state and begin moving towards Berlin, Germany in a straight line with the apparent ability to move through anything in it's path."
After I posted the draft to the forums and in chat it was this exact line that drew a lot of criticism and was quickly changed to what it currently reads.
You are right that this SCP holds little mystery, you know exactly what it is and I still feel I went information overload on the experimentation phase but again in the original draft it held only one experiment, this is experiment 04 in the current version, which stopped at the line "SCP-3390 failed to move in the predicted direction and was lost from view for ██ hours after driving through ████████."
With the last paragraph blanked out and ending with the words "Further testing to be postponed pending recovery"
This was again held to much criticism and more information was added to much approval.

This SCP was my first one so I tended to hold any criticisms I received in the first stages as gospel. I had originally intended to use this as a learning experience with the whole thing bleeding into a number of other SCP's to introduce the character of Dr Wintergreen who would have headed up an underfunded Historical department specialising in safe to Euclid First World War SCP's.
Unfortunately I got bummed out on the general contradictory nature of what the community actually wants and after an over reaction from the staff regarding a potential copyright issue of a 100 year old postcard I gave up and haven't really given much regard to the Foundation until recently.
I aim to partially re-write this as a later stage.

item.title: This is a good idea, but...
item.content:encoded:
‘Calculating its trajectory and taking history into account, speculation is that its destination is Berlin, Germany.’

There are a lot of issues with this scp, but this bit sums up a lot. The foundation does not need to take history into account or speculate. Start the tank from two or three different places and, viola, it always heads towards Berlin. You can’t create mystery just by suggesting there’s speculation.

Instead, I imagine this is meant to point the reader towards the ‘relevant hostory’ bit, which is neat and all, but way overlong and leaves nothing to the reader.

Similarly, you say the tank isn’t stopped by materials that would appropriately inhibit the tank’s historical counterparts. What about the tank itself? Wouldn’t they be testing what this anomolous object, which may be from the moon or the future or Xanth, itself is made of?

There’s no room for mystery here, just a cute premise you’re flogging us with. SCP’s work by allowing the reader to draw conclusions. This is just a data dump.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since I keep getting encouragement regarding this SCP I am considering a partial re-write when I have more time.
Thank you for all your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some punctuation issues scattered throughout the article. Also, the inclusion of the footnote specifying not to use it to learn French is not something that would need to be included. First, it's unprofessional; second, it's a possible containment breach and potential loss of valuable assets by tampering with the minds of expensive, highly trained scientists. Just like you don't play around in a high school science lab, you shouldn't be using SCPs for personal gain or entertainment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the second incident log, it repeats 'their' twice. Just a little thing that needs to be fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Problematic on several fronts.

1. SCP-3390 is NOT to be used by researchers to quickly learn the French language.

Strikes off to me as lol-Foundation. Such a thing won't be considered in a professional environment. It's like a pharmacist taking experimental Viagra, with little idea on what will actually happen to said person.

Overall, the actual anomalies are very boring. It compels people to operate it, and act as an effective caricature of French patriotism. And that's all there is to the SCP, really. There is also a bulk of information that are not interesting, particularly most of the Relevant History. Focus on things directly pertinent to the SCP, not just the general type of tanks.

As for the experiment logs, the current formatting is problematic. It does not distinguish the experiment's purpose and the various components needed to conduct the experiment, which makes reading confusing. Also, most of them are effectively reiterations of whatever you have already told us in the article. The experiments do little in offering new information that would change our understanding of the SCP.

And the supposed new information given (SCP-3390's self-regeneration capabilities) is not a suitable direction. Adding a new anomalous effect at the end without context does not change our understanding of the SCP. That would be better executed if you end on a note that makes readers question the assumptions they made at the beginning of the article.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
You have not provided an original source. The postcard is a physical copy of an image, the postcard seller does not have original image rights to the image on the postcard. They can sell their own physical copy, but don't have intellectual property rights on the image itself. The image is almost certainly public domain, but without an original image source we cannot be sure that this isn't a reproduction under more recent and stringent copyright terms.

Until you can find and provide us with a source proving the image is compatible with the site's image policy you may not readd it to the article and I will be removing it again now. Assuming you find such a source, please contact either myself or LilyFlower before to add the image back to your article.

In the mean time here are 5 different images to choose from on wikimedia commons. I would suggest you save yourself some time and trouble and just use one of these: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Char_2C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can give you some screencaps but I am going to have to censor out the e-mail address and the guys name.

I think it's fine to censor out the email address, though I'm not sure how attribution would work if we don't have (at least part of) a name. I'll mention this to our Licensing staff to confirm.

I note the tumblr and pinterest pages have the watermarks intact, would this suffice as an original source?

…probably not, unless someone on one of those sites owns the image? Again, I'll double-check.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can give you some screencaps but I am going to have to censor out the e-mail address and the guys name.
I note the tumblr and pinterest pages have the watermarks intact, would this suffice as an original source?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The postcard was for sale and after contacting the seller I was able to acquire the image used here without the watermark.

Do you have some form of confirmation of the communication with the seller? An email or other online message?

Since the card was sold Delcampe.net have removed the listing from their servers and ,as far as I am aware, this SCP current remains the only image of it online.

A quick reverse-image search with Google indicates that there are versions of this present on tumblr and pinterest: https://www.pinterest.com/pin/460352393148188819/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have been asked to provide a source on the image shown in this document.
This proves something of a problem.
The original image I used was posted on a website called Delcampe.net, which is kind of like a shit ebay, and was the image of a vintage cigarette card. They had a tiny watermark across the bottom of the image.
The postcard was for sale and after contacting the seller I was able to acquire the image used here without the watermark.
The Photograph is close to 100 years old, I have no idea who took it nor did the person selling the postcard.

Since the card was sold Delcampe.net have removed the listing from their servers and ,as far as I am aware, this SCP current remains the only image of it online.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: As the 48 hours have passed with no reply from the author, the images have been removed amd the author sent a PM. They are free to add them back in if they can provide an original source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They were assisted in their removal from existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That helps a lot, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While there are some grammer issues, this one is well pretty unique in both its concept and its formatting, so I give it a +1.

Though, some grammer I would definitely recommend changing is:

manufactured 1919 developed by the French Army in the post World War One period.

As this sentence comes early on in the article, it may turn some people off. So I propose changing it to either:
"manufactured in 1919, and developed by the French Army in the post World War One period."
Or:
"manufactured and developed by the French Army circa 1919, in the post World War One period."

Hope this helps in some way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, what happened here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was on the fence until the footnote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens to the D's! Stupid black boxes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *casually hiding tip-ex behind his back" I don't know what you're talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was told to not do this. I was lied to. I shall fix this before it hits -14 and try to claw my way back up to a respectable 0.
Thankyou

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It still does have a site link down in the right corner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good idea with solid execution, let down by poor formatting. No-vote.

Much better now. Not perfect, but well worth an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You need to hit Enter/Return twice after each paragraph, not once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed it. Worked in drafts no idea why it suddenly went all crazy eyes on me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, please note that your image is broken, and the image you are trying to use has a site link. Please, use another image or dont use any.
